stations.title: Dyad
stations.blurb: The fundamental node constructing the entire Logic Graph. It is a struct only containing two fields: type and value. The type explains how the value should be interpreted. It is named Dyad because it contains exactly two fields

stations.title: Type
stations.blurb: Everything in Logos has a type and is used to explain the value. The type of type is type itself

stations.title: Token
stations.blurb: Lexer creates tokens and the parser consumes tokens. Each token contains an identity that is identified by the lexer but is not instantiated yet. The parser is what instantiates the identities in the tokens and removes the tokens.

stations.title: Lexer
stations.blurb: The lexer is based on a trie just that each node can also be a regex pattern so that nearly all string identifications you want to do could be represented. Since its a trie its both fast but also mutable. The default way to identify anything in any string of any language is through this global lexer. It resolves multiple equal strings by checking what context in the Logic Graph it is declared.

stations.title: Create all fundamental identities
stations.blurb: In Logos everything is an identity. In the seed other identities are needed as well like struct, i32, f64, ., &, @, (, +, language, :=, for, if, while, fn, -, and '«'. Its not necessary to make an issue for each identity as many will be made together in batches that are unknown beforehand.

stations.title: mut, pub and other (new) rw gates
stations.blurb: These aren't strictly needed in the seed but they help a lot with safety since everything should only be readable by default and also not everything should be able to read everything. Most likely everything in the seed would need read every other part of the seed and there must be a explicit rw gate for this, just like mut and pub.

stations.title: The «?» identity
stations.blurb: «?» is really important to state what is unknown. In any possible state of Logos in the future there will always be unknown things, which means there will always be things to improve, and that is why an explicit identity of what is unknown is needed. When the self-identification system gets made it will start being able to solve what should be in the «?» spots. An example is «sin(x)² + cos(x)² == ?».

stations.title: Parser
stations.blurb: The parser needs to know what each token from the lexer means and therefore all tokens needs to have a defined type.

stations.title: Compiler
stations.blurb: Compiling the Logic Graph to binary code. Only functions can be compiled. A set of identities are leafs which represents the IR primitives.

stations.title: Interpreter
stations.blurb: The interpreter runs any give Logic Graph function without compiling anything, but it relies on at least the leaves being compiled, because the leaves is the "ground truth" in the Logic Graph and everything is fundamentally defined in through leaves at the bottom but the leaves isn't defined in other identities. They are defined but their definitions lies in the compiled and how it converts the leaves to machine-code.

stations.title: Proof system
stations.blurb: Creating proof system with universe, predicates and proofs.
